scenario: A team wants to expose a DMN decision as an API endpoint (call directly without BPMN). Team wonders if Camunda 8 supports standalone decision invocation.
question: Standalone decision invocation via API?

options.text: DMN decisions deployed in Camunda 8 can be evaluated standalone without a BPMN Business Rule Task. mdx:32-36, accepting decisionDefinitionId and variables in the request body. md:751-781 provides the same capability. When invoked by decision ID, the latest deployed version is used. No BPMN process instance is required; the evaluation instance is still recorded in Operate. Use cases include validation APIs and testing pipelines.

Decisions deployed to Camunda 8 are first-class resources that can be evaluated independently of any process instance. The REST API exposes a dedicated decision evaluation endpoint (POST /decisions/{decisionDefinitionKey}/evaluate or similar) that invokes the DMN engine directly with provided input variables.

No BPMN process required. Useful for decision testing, external system integration, and decision-as-a-service patterns. The Camunda Client SDKs also wrap this capability. Each evaluation returns the decision output and all matched rules per the configured hit policy.

Using an external DMN engine separate from Camunda to evaluate decisions is possible but introduces unnecessary complexity and duplication. Camunda 8 already has a built-in DMN engine that evaluates decisions deployed to the cluster, accessible via REST and gRPC APIs. Deploying and maintaining a second DMN engine creates synchronization challenges, version drift risks, and additional operational overhead. The native Camunda DMN evaluation endpoint eliminates the need for an external engine in the described scenario.

a.text: Incorrect. Standalone DMN evaluation via Camunda 8 API: API endpoints (Camunda 8.8): REST: POST /decision-definitions/evaluation (Orchestration Cluster API). gRPC: EvaluateDecision RPC on Zeebe gateway. Returns: synchronous decision output. Example REST call: `http POST /decision-definitions/evaluation Content-Type: application/json Authorization: Bearer { "decisionDefinitionId": "creditCheck", "variables": { "customerId": "C-100", "loanAmount": 5000 } } Response: json { "decisionId": "creditCheck", "decisionVersion": 3, "result": { "approved": true, "limit": 10000, "interestRate": 0.05 }, "decisionInstanceId": "di-12345" } ` Use cases: Use case 1: Validation microservice: External app needs to validate input against business rules. Calls DMN endpoint; gets decision. No process needed; just rule evaluation. Use case 2: Scoring service: Risk scoring exposed as API. Apps consume; route customers based on score. Use case 3: Embedded in non-Camunda apps: Microservice architecture; DMN engine as one service. Business rules centralised; multiple consumers. Use case 4: A/B testing rules: Deploy two decision versions. Route requests; analyse outcomes. Camunda Decisions become testable as services. vs BPMN-embedded: BPMN Business Rule Task: decision is part of a process flow. Standalone: decision evaluated outside any process. Audit / instance tracking: both produce decision instances recorded in Operate (per Set 13 Q29). Auth: SaaS: OAuth client credentials; cluster endpoint. Self-Managed: Identity / Keycloak or token-based per setup. Performance: Synchronous: typical evaluation is fast (ms-level for simple decisions). Caching: decisions deterministic given inputs → cacheable if inputs repeat. Concurrency: API scales per cluster capacity. Decision input validation: API validates input types (per declared input column types). Mismatches return error response. Result structure: Single output decision (one output column): result is the value. Multiple outputs: result is an object. COLLECT: result is a list. COLLECT with aggregator (per Q23): result is the aggregated scalar. Operational considerations: Decision deployment: Deploy DMN files via API or Web Modeler. Each deployment creates a new version. API calls reference by decisionId; latest version typically. Versioning: Specify version in request: pin to specific version. Default: latest. Decision instance retention: Each invocation logged (per Set 13 Q29). Useful for audit / replay. Best practices: Define clear input contracts: decisions are public API. Version explicitly when breaking: avoid surprises. Monitor decision invocations: latency, error rate. Document for consumers: input/output schemas, error responses.
